Ayah 1–15 · linked video: 01. Az-Zukhruf (Ayah 1-15)

Surah Az-Zukhruf: Commentary on Ayahs 1-15

Ayah 1

حمٓ

This surah begins with one of the disconnected letters that open several chapters of the Quran. The surah is paired in many ways with the one that follows it, Surah Ad-Dukhan, which becomes clear even from their opening passages. Both surahs share thematic connections, and as we progress through Az-Zukhruf, we’ll explore fascinating insights into the psychology of disbelievers and what they expect from Allah ﷻ.

Ayah 2

وَٱلْكِتَـٰبِ ٱلْمُبِينِ

Allah ﷻ takes an oath by the Book that is absolutely clear and clarifying. This oath establishes the foundation for what follows: the reality and nature of divine revelation.

Ayah 3

إِنَّا جَعَلْنَـٰهُ قُرْءَٰنًا عَرَبِيًّا لَّعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ

The oath leads to its purpose: We have made it an Arabic Quran so that you may understand. The word جَعَلْنَـٰهُ is significant here. In Arabic, جَعَلَ means “to make” in a specific way—to transform something that already exists into something else. This differs from خَلَقَ , which means to create from nothing.

When Allah ﷻ says “We made it,” He’s indicating a transformation. The original revelation exists in its divine form with Allah ﷻ, in a language beyond human comprehension. Allah ﷻ then transformed it into Arabic so we could understand it. It’s similar to saying “I made you a teacher”—you already existed, and then you were transformed into a teacher. The revelation existed in its original divine form, and Allah ﷻ made it into an Arabic Quran.

The purpose is clear: لَّعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ so that you may understand, so that you may use your intellect. The Quran came in a language people could comprehend and engage with intellectually.

Ayah 4

وَإِنَّهُۥ فِىٓ أُمِّ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لَدَيْنَا لَعَلِىٌّ حَكِيمٌ

The original form of this revelation exists فِىٓ أُمِّ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ —in the Mother of the Book. This is also called Al-Lawh Al-Mahfudh (the Preserved Tablet), Al-Kitab Al-Maknun (the Hidden Book), and other names. It’s that original Scripture that resides with Allah ﷻ always, the treasure from which He reveals all revelations to humanity.

This original revelation is لَدَيْنَا —in Our possession, لَعَلِىٌّ حَكِيمٌ —truly elevated and full of wisdom. Notice these are the names of Allah ﷻ used in previous surahs—Al-Ali and Al-Hakeem—and now they describe the Book itself that rests with Him.

Ayah 5

أَفَنَضْرِبُ عَنكُمُ ٱلذِّكْرَ صَفْحًا أَن كُنتُمْ قَوْمًا مُّسْرِفِينَ

This ayah presents a striking rhetorical question. Should We then turn away the reminder from you, just abandon it, simply because you have been an excessive, transgressive people?

The word صَفْحًا literally means “to turn a page,” to turn away from someone. It also carries the meaning of forgiveness or excusing. Allah ﷻ is asking: Do you think We’re just going to walk away from you with an excuse? And what’s that excuse? That you’ve been مُّسْرِفِينَ —an excessive, transgressive nation for a long time.

This captures a mindset that exists even today. Sometimes people think certain individuals or communities are beyond the reach of Islam’s message. They’re “too far gone,” “too elite,” or living a lifestyle so different that there’s no point in sharing the message with them. But Allah ﷻ demolishes this thinking. He’s saying: Just because you’ve been excessively criminal doesn’t mean We abandon the message. This reminder isn’t only for the righteous—it’s precisely for those who need it most.

This ayah powerfully defines the audience of the Quran. Yes, it’s for those who willingly sit and study it, but these ayahs are addressing the unwilling, those who might say, “Why even bother with us? We’re too far out there. This revelation doesn’t apply to us.” Allah ﷻ responds: Your transgression is exactly why you need this message, not a reason to withhold it.

The prophets understood this. They went to public spaces—marketplaces, town halls, places where life happens—and delivered their message. These weren’t religious sanctuaries; they were places where people would say, “This isn’t the place for religion.” But that’s precisely where the prophets went.

We often live in a bubble, surrounded by decent, religious people. Our idea of someone struggling might be someone who missed Fajr. But there’s tremendous darkness in this world, sometimes right in our own neighborhoods. Allah ﷻ sent us as ambassadors of Islam to reach those places, to bring people out of those depths.

Ayah 6

وَكَمْ أَرْسَلْنَا مِن نَّبِىٍّ فِى ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ

How many messengers did We send among the earlier generations? This question establishes a pattern that has repeated throughout history.

Ayah 7

وَمَا يَأْتِيهِم مِّن نَّبِىٍّ إِلَّا كَانُوا۟ بِهِۦ يَسْتَهْزِءُونَ

No messenger came to them except that they mocked him, they ridiculed him, they made fun of him. This is the consistent response across generations and nations.

Ayah 8

فَأَهْلَكْنَآ أَشَدَّ مِنْهُم بَطْشًا وَمَضَىٰ مَثَلُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ

We destroyed those who were more powerful than them in their ability to seize and punish. The word بَطْشًا means to grab, to squeeze, to punish with force. Those previous nations had tremendous power, yet Allah ﷻ destroyed them. And the example of those earlier peoples has already passed—their stories are recorded, their consequences documented, their lessons preserved.

Ayah 9

وَلَئِن سَأَلْتَهُم مَّنْ خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ لَيَقُولُنَّ خَلَقَهُنَّ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ

If you were to ask them, “Who created the heavens and the earth?” they would certainly say, “They were created by the Almighty, the All-Knowing.” Even the disbelievers of Makkah acknowledged Allah’s ﷻ creative power. They recognized Him as Al-Aziz (the Ultimate Authority, the Almighty) and Al-Aleem (the One who knows everything). Their problem wasn’t denying Allah’s ﷻ existence or creative power—it was refusing to submit to His authority in their daily lives.

Ayah 10

ٱلَّذِى جَعَلَ لَكُمُ ٱلْأَرْضَ مَهْدًا وَجَعَلَ لَكُمْ فِيهَا سُبُلًا لَّعَلَّكُمْ تَهْتَدُونَ

Notice جَعَلَ appears again—He made the earth for you مَهْدًا , a place of comfort, like a cradle or smooth flooring. The earth wasn’t always this way; it was transformed for our benefit. He made pathways in it—routes through mountains, roads across lands—so that you may find guidance, so you can commit yourselves to the right road.

When there are multiple pathways carved between mountains and across terrain, you can navigate and find your way. This is both literal and metaphorical guidance.

Ayah 11

وَٱلَّذِى نَزَّلَ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَآءًۢ بِقَدَرٍ فَأَنشَرْنَا بِهِۦ بَلْدَةً مَّيْتًا ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ تُخْرَجُونَ

He is the One who sent water down from the sky بِقَدَرٍ —with precise measure, exact calculation. Not too much, not too little. Then by means of that water, We raised and revived بَلْدَةً مَّيْتًا —a land that was dead, a land that was characterized by death itself, where the very earth reminded you of lifelessness.

Then comes the powerful connection: كَذَٰلِكَ تُخْرَجُونَ —that’s exactly how you will be brought out. Just as dead earth springs to life with water, you will be brought out from your graves on the Day of Resurrection. This is among the Quran’s most elegant arguments for resurrection—observable in nature, undeniable in its logic.

Ayah 12

وَٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ ٱلْأَزْوَٰجَ كُلَّهَا وَجَعَلَ لَكُم مِّنَ ٱلْفُلْكِ وَٱلْأَنْعَـٰمِ مَا تَرْكَبُونَ

The One who created all the pairs, all the spouses of creation, and made for you from ships and cattle things that you ride. Notice Allah ﷻ places ships and cattle in the same category. We might think, “We designed ships,” but actually, the engineering of ships was inspired by Allah ﷻ. We learn from the story of Prophet Nuh (AS) that ship-building came through divine revelation. The knowledge of how to construct vessels that float—this is something Allah ﷻ taught humanity.

Ayah 13

لِتَسْتَوُۥا۟ عَلَىٰ ظُهُورِهِۦ ثُمَّ تَذْكُرُوا۟ نِعْمَةَ رَبِّكُمْ إِذَا ٱسْتَوَيْتُمْ عَلَيْهِ وَتَقُولُوا۟ سُبْحَـٰنَ ٱلَّذِى سَخَّرَ لَنَا هَـٰذَا وَمَا كُنَّا لَهُۥ مُقْرِنِينَ

So that you can settle yourselves upon their backs. The word لِتَسْتَوُۥا۟ carries the image of a king taking his throne, settling into his proper place. When you ride a horse or camel, you climb up, adjust yourself, find your position—that’s istawa. When you board a ship, you find your seat, settle in. When you get into your car, you adjust the seat, move it forward or back, find your comfort—that’s all istawa.

Then, after you’ve settled, تَذْكُرُوا۟ نِعْمَةَ رَبِّكُمْ —you should remember and mention the favor of your Master upon you.

And you should say: سُبْحَـٰنَ ٱلَّذِى سَخَّرَ لَنَا هَـٰذَا—“How perfect is the One who has subjected this to our use, who has placed this under our control.”

This is the dua we’re taught to recite when mounting any form of transportation. Many of us learned it as children—our parents made us recite it before the car would start moving.

But notice something profound: Allah ﷻ says He created these rides لِتَسْتَوُۥا۟ (so that you can settle on them) and also لِتَذْكُرُوا۟ (so that you can remember and make this dua). The reason He created transportation isn’t just for convenience—it’s so this dua can exist. We don’t have rides so we can make dua; we make dua because that’s why we have rides. The dua is the purpose; the transportation is the means to that spiritual end.

Now, here’s another insight: rides and pride are intimately connected. Back in the day, a man on a fine horse carried himself differently than a man on a donkey. Today, someone driving a luxury car has a different demeanor than someone in an old vehicle. The person with the expensive car makes sure it’s washed, detailed, noticed. He might add modifications—not for himself, since he’s inside the vehicle, but so others will see it. Some remove the muffler so the engine roars and announces their presence.

People with private jets, yachts—these represent different levels of wealth and pride. Transportation has always been tied to status and ego.

That’s precisely why this dua is so important. سُبْحَـٰنَ ٱلَّذِى سَخَّرَ لَنَا هَـٰذَا—“How perfect is the One who subjected this to us”—is a statement of humility. The moment you enter your vehicle, you’re commanded to humble yourself, because transportation is one of the primary places where human pride manifests.

وَمَا كُنَّا لَهُۥ مُقْرِنِينَ —“and we could never have control over it on our own.” We were never in control. We are not in control. If a horse goes wild, you can’t stop it. If your brakes fail at 80 mph, there’s nothing you can do. Yes, it’s a German import, Japanese engineering, highly reliable—but reliable only to a point.

Your car has thousands of moving parts. Anything can fail. A tire blows out at 85 mph—you’re not in control anymore. This dua is an acknowledgment that these creatures, these machines, are not truly under our power. They function by Allah’s ﷻ permission and design, and that can change in an instant.

Ayah 14

وَإِنَّآ إِلَىٰ رَبِّنَا لَمُنقَلِبُونَ

And indeed, we are going to return to our Master. This is a powerful statement to make when embarking on a journey. You’re leaving home, planning to return home—but you might not. One thing is absolutely certain: you will return to Allah ﷻ.

It’s a profound acknowledgment of humility and the fragile nature of life. When we travel in our cars, on planes, on ships—these are moments where death is close. If you’ve ever truly reflected on the fact that you’re sitting in a piece of metal hurtling at speeds never before known in human history, alongside other pieces of metal, mere feet apart, all traveling at these incredible velocities—it’s actually terrifying.

If people from previous generations saw us driving on highways, they would think we’re insane. “You’re going to get killed! Why would you do that?” We look at daredevils jumping from planes or bungee jumping and think, “I would never do that—it’s crazy!” But people of older generations would look at our daily commute the same way.

How many accidents happen every day? How many people die in traffic every single day? Yet we get back in our cars without hesitation. That’s the nature of modern life.

But this dua keeps us grounded: وَإِنَّآ إِلَىٰ رَبِّنَا لَمُنقَلِبُونَ—we’re going on this journey, but ultimately, certainly, we’re returning to Allah ﷻ. Whether we make it to our earthly destination or not, our final destination is already determined.

Ayah 15

وَجَعَلُوا۟ لَهُۥ مِنْ عِبَادِهِۦ جُزْءًا ۚ إِنَّ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ لَكَفُورٌ مُّبِينٌ

And they made for Him, from among His own slaves, a portion. The language here is complex but the meaning is clear: the disbelievers attributed parts of creation—specifically, they claimed that angels were Allah’s ﷻ daughters, and they assigned gender and relationship to divine matters that are far above such attributions.

The ayah concludes: إِنَّ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ لَكَفُورٌ مُّبِينٌ —indeed, the human being is clearly ungrateful. After all the blessings detailed in the previous ayahs—the earth made comfortable, pathways carved out, water sent with precision, ships and animals provided for transport, the ability to travel and return safely—after all of this, human beings respond with ingratitude and false attribution.

This sets the stage for the themes that continue throughout the surah: the blessings of Allah ﷻ, the consistent human response of denial and ingratitude, and the inevitable consequences that follow.

Ayah 15–36 · linked video: 02. Az-Zukhruf (Ayah 15-36)

Surah Az-Zukhruf: Ayahs 15-36

Ayah 15

وَجَعَلُوا۟ لَهُۥ مِنْ عِبَادِهِۦ جُزْءًا ۚ إِنَّ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ لَكَفُورٌ مُّبِينٌ

And they attributed to Him from among His servants a portion. Indeed, mankind is clearly ungrateful.

This ayah addresses one of the most fundamental distortions in human understanding of Allah ﷻ. The word جُزْءًا carries profound significance here - it means “a portion” or “a part.” When we say a child is part of their parents, we recognize that physically, biologically, they carry portions of both the mother and father. The child emerges from their very bodies. So when someone is called another person’s child, the implication is that they are literally a part of them.

This is why Allah ﷻ uses the word juz’ to describe their claim. Out of His slaves, they took some and declared them a part of God. This directly addresses Christian theology’s claim that Jesus (AS) is part of God - that the divine nature consists of three parts: the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit. They describe Jesus (AS) as part God, the Holy Ghost as a part of God. This is the corruption Allah ﷻ is exposing.

The ayah concludes with a devastating assessment: إِنَّ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ لَكَفُورٌ مُّبِينٌ - indeed, mankind is extremely ungrateful. The human being is truly kafur mubin - openly, clearly, undeniably ungrateful. Despite all of Allah’s favors, despite His obvious signs, humans have the audacity to attribute partners to Him and distort His nature.

Ayah 16

أَمِ ٱتَّخَذَ مِمَّا يَخْلُقُ بَنَاتٍ وَأَصْفَىٰكُم بِٱلْبَنِينَ

Or has He taken, out of what He creates, daughters and chosen you for sons?

Now Allah ﷻ specifically addresses the Arab mushrikeen and their twisted logic. The question is rhetorical and cuts to the heart of their hypocrisy: Did He take for Himself daughters from His creation while He purified you, preferring for you to have sons?

This isn’t just a matter of shirk - it’s about what you attribute to Allah ﷻ versus what you want for yourself. They despised having daughters and loved having sons. So in their distorted worldview, they assigned to Allah ﷻ what they themselves hated (daughters) while keeping for themselves what they loved (sons). The absurdity and disrespect are staggering.

Ayah 17

وَإِذَا بُشِّرَ أَحَدُهُم بِمَا ضَرَبَ لِلرَّحْمَـٰنِ مَثَلًا ظَلَّ وَجْهُهُۥ مُسْوَدًّا وَهُوَ كَظِيمٌ

And when one of them is given good tidings of that which he attributes to the Most Merciful in comparison, his face becomes dark, and he suppresses grief.

Allah ﷻ now exposes their hypocrisy with devastating precision. When one of them receives the “good news” - congratulations! Just like the god you worship, you too have been blessed with a daughter! - watch what happens. His face turns black, ظَلَّ وَجْهُهُۥ مُسْوَدًّا, as if a dark cloud is hanging over it. The moment he hears it’s a girl, depression and embarrassment overwhelm him.

وَهُوَ كَظِيمٌ - and he is suppressing rage, swallowing it down. He’s holding it in, not wanting others to see how devastated he is, how ashamed he feels that it’s a girl when it should have been a boy.

This ayah addresses the mushrikeen who had committed shirk regarding daughters, believing angels were Allah’s daughters. The news of having a daughter was utterly depressing to them. But here’s the tragic reality: this jahiliyyah attitude, though harshly criticized in Allah’s Book, still exists in parts of the Muslim world today. People have daughters and become depressed - “another one” - as if it’s a calamity. They don’t even consider what Allah ﷻ described in the previous surah, where daughters were mentioned as a gift first, then sons: يَهَبُ لِمَن يَشَآءُ إِنَـٰثًا - He grants to whom He wills females.

The Prophet ﷺ delivered one of the most beautiful hadith on this subject: Whoever has three daughters or three sisters and is good to them will enter Jannah without exception. Three daughters, three sisters - that’s your ticket to Paradise. The only complication is being good to them.

Ayah 18

أَوَمَن يُنَشَّؤُا۟ فِى ٱلْحِلْيَةِ وَهُوَ فِى ٱلْخِصَامِ غَيْرُ مُبِينٍ

So is one brought up in ornaments while being during conflict unarticulate [attributed to Allah]?

Now Allah ﷻ comments on their shallow mentality about girls. They looked at girls and thought: What good are they? The ayah says: أَوَمَن يُنَشَّؤُا۟ فِى ٱلْحِلْيَةِ - the one who is brought up in jewelry, in ornaments? All she cares about are her little earrings, necklaces, bracelets. When she’s little, she plays with pretend jewelry. As she grows, she wants real jewelry - decoration, glitter, trinkets.

The Arabs observed this natural tendency in girls and concluded dismissively: This is all they’re good for. وَهُوَ فِى ٱلْخِصَامِ غَيْرُ مُبِينٍ - and when it comes to arguing, to fighting and conflict, she cannot articulate herself clearly.

Notice the use of the masculine pronoun هُوَ even though we’re discussing females. Arabic allows this when speaking generally. The Arabs valued boys primarily for their ability to fight on their behalf. But this girl? When people start yelling at her, she can’t say anything. She gets scared and shrinks back. She’s shy - every time you raise your voice, she trembles. How is she going to defend or help? That was their attitude toward daughters.

This, Allah ﷻ is saying, is why you get so depressed when a daughter is born.

Ayah 19

وَجَعَلُوا۟ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةَ ٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ عِبَـٰدُ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ إِنَـٰثًا ۚ أَشَهِدُوا۟ خَلْقَهُمْ ۚ سَتُكْتَبُ شَهَـٰدَتُهُمْ وَيُسْـَٔلُونَ

And they have made the angels, who are servants of the Most Merciful, females. Did they witness their creation? Their testimony will be recorded, and they will be questioned.

This transition is remarkably powerful. The previous ayah commented on the weakness they attributed to girls in their minds - girls who play with jewelry, who can’t even argue back, who appear powerless. And then they claim angels are female.

Wait - so angels are weak? They can’t do anything? You’re picking the wrong beings to mess with. The angels who are عِبَـٰدُ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ - the devoted servants of Ar-Rahman - you’re calling them female as an insult?

أَشَهِدُوا۟ خَلْقَهُمْ - Did they witness their creation? Were they present when the angels were made? سَتُكْتَبُ شَهَـٰدَتُهُمْ - Their testimony will be written down. But who will write it down? The angels themselves. وَيُسْـَٔلُونَ - And they will be questioned. But who does the questioning on Judgment Day? Angels.

So these “mighty” angels they dismissed as weak will be the very ones recording their false testimony and interrogating them. The irony is devastating: You thought we looked weak? We’ll see about that on the Day you face us.

Ayah 20

وَقَالُوا۟ لَوْ شَآءَ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ مَا عَبَدْنَـٰهُم ۗ مَّا لَهُم بِذَٰلِكَ مِنْ عِلْمٍ ۖ إِنْ هُمْ إِلَّا يَخْرُصُونَ

And they said, “If the Most Merciful had willed, we would not have worshiped them.” They have no knowledge of that. They are not but falsifying.

Now they employ a different tactic - theological fatalism. They say, “If Ar-Rahman had willed it, we wouldn’t have worshiped these angels or saints or others.” In other words, this is predestined. God wanted this to happen, so we’re doing it.

مَّا لَهُم بِذَٰلِكَ مِنْ عِلْمٍ - They have no knowledge whatsoever about what they’re saying. When they invoke divine will and predestination, they’re speaking without any basis in reality.

إِنْ هُمْ إِلَّا يَخْرُصُونَ - They are doing nothing but خَرْص - making wild guesses and presenting them as facts. Khars refers to someone who looks at a palm tree and declares confidently, “This tree has exactly 827 dates. I can tell.” No, you can’t. But he speaks like he knows precisely what he’s talking about when in reality he’s just blowing hot air.

A person who does this is called a kharras - someone making things up out of thin air but packaging it compellingly. Like a smooth-talking car salesman, like Wall Street analysts who sound authoritative with their terminology and projections but are really just speculating wildly. “The stock’s going up, guaranteed, you’ll miss out!” You listen thinking, “Wow, this person really knows their stuff.” No, they don’t. It’s all empty talk.

These people are kharrasun. First, they’re skilled talkers - eloquent and persuasive. Second, they use predestination as their justification for wrongdoing. “If Allah ﷻ wanted, I would have been Muslim. I wouldn’t have done shirk.” You’ll find people like this who seem knowledgeable and well-spoken, using divine decree to rationalize their rebellion.

Ayah 21

أَمْ ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُمْ كِتَـٰبًا مِّن قَبْلِهِۦ فَهُم بِهِۦ مُسْتَمْسِكُونَ

Or have We given them a book before it to which they are adhering?

Allah ﷻ challenges them with another rhetorical question. In the previous surah, He asked: أَمْ لَهُمْ شُرَكَآءُ - Do they have partners who legislated religion for them? Now He asks: Do you have a book that We gave you from before this Quran - فَهُم بِهِۦ مُسْتَمْسِكُونَ - that you’re holding onto so tightly?

The word مُسْتَمْسِك means to grip something with absolute conviction, refusing to let go. So where’s this divine book you’re clinging to that justifies your beliefs?

Ayah 22

بَلْ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّا وَجَدْنَآ ءَابَآءَنَا عَلَىٰٓ أُمَّةٍ وَإِنَّا عَلَىٰٓ ءَاثَـٰرِهِم مُّهْتَدُونَ

Rather, they say, “Indeed, we found our fathers upon a religion, and we are in their footsteps guided.”

بَلْ - Rather, no. They don’t have a book. Instead, they say: إِنَّا وَجَدْنَآ ءَابَآءَنَا عَلَىٰٓ أُمَّةٍ - We found our forefathers committed to an ummah, a nation, a legacy, a single direction and focus.

وَإِنَّا عَلَىٰٓ ءَاثَـٰرِهِم مُّهْتَدُونَ - And we are going to be rightly guided by following their traces and footsteps. True guidance, in their view, belongs to walking in the ruins and traditions of their ancestors.

Ayah 23

وَكَذَٰلِكَ مَآ أَرْسَلْنَا مِن قَبْلِكَ فِى قَرْيَةٍ مِّن نَّذِيرٍ إِلَّا قَالَ مُتْرَفُوهَآ إِنَّا وَجَدْنَآ ءَابَآءَنَا عَلَىٰٓ أُمَّةٍ وَإِنَّا عَلَىٰٓ ءَاثَـٰرِهِم مُّقْتَدُونَ

And similarly, We did not send before you any warner into a city except that its affluent said, “Indeed, we found our fathers upon a religion, and we are, in their footsteps, following.”

This is the pattern throughout history. وَكَذَٰلِكَ - And just like this, مَآ أَرْسَلْنَا مِن قَبْلِكَ فِى قَرْيَةٍ مِّن نَّذِيرٍ - We did not send into any town any warner before you, إِلَّا قَالَ مُتْرَفُوهَآ - except that the same thing happened: the mutrafun spoke up.

Who are the mutrafun? The elite, those deluded and blinded by their wealth and luxury. They’re the ones who always resist. And what do they say? The exact same thing: إِنَّا وَجَدْنَآ ءَابَآءَنَا عَلَىٰٓ أُمَّةٍ - We found our forefathers committed to this nation’s way. وَإِنَّا عَلَىٰٓ ءَاثَـٰرِهِم مُّقْتَدُونَ - And we are going to closely follow their footsteps, their legacies, commit ourselves to their way and lifestyle.

This is the timeless excuse of those who resist prophetic guidance.

Ayah 24

۞ قَـٰلَ أَوَلَوْ جِئْتُكُم بِأَهْدَىٰ مِمَّا وَجَدتُّمْ عَلَيْهِ ءَابَآءَكُمْ ۖ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّا بِمَآ أُرْسِلْتُم بِهِۦ كَـٰفِرُونَ

[Each warner] said, “Even if I brought you better guidance than that upon which you found your fathers?” They said, “Indeed we, in that with which you were sent, are disbelievers.”

The warner responds with a simple, devastating question: أَوَلَوْ جِئْتُكُم بِأَهْدَىٰ - Even if I brought you something more guided, better guidance - مِمَّا وَجَدتُّمْ عَلَيْهِ ءَابَآءَكُمْ - than what you found your fathers committed to? Just compare it. Look at what I’m offering versus what your ancestors practiced.

Their response? قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّا بِمَآ أُرْسِلْتُم بِهِۦ كَـٰفِرُونَ - We are in complete denial of whatever you have been sent with. They won’t even consider it.

Ayah 25

فَٱنتَقَمْنَا مِنْهُمْ ۖ فَٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلْمُكَذِّبِينَ

So we took retribution from them; then see how was the end of the deniers.

فَٱنتَقَمْنَا مِنْهُمْ - Then We took vengeance from them. When they persisted in their rejection, divine retribution came. فَٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلْمُكَذِّبِينَ - Then look for yourself, examine history: What was the outcome of those who called the messengers liars?

Ayah 26

وَإِذْ قَالَ إِبْرَٰهِيمُ لِأَبِيهِ وَقَوْمِهِۦٓ إِنَّنِى بَرَآءٌ مِّمَّا تَعْبُدُونَ

And [mention, O Muhammad], when Ibrahim said to his father and his people, “Indeed, I am disassociated from that which you worship.”

Now Allah ﷻ presents the counter-example - someone who broke from ancestral tradition for truth. وَإِذْ قَالَ إِبْرَٰهِيمُ لِأَبِيهِ وَقَوْمِهِۦٓ - When Ibrahim (AS) said to his father and his nation.

إِنَّنِى بَرَآءٌ - I am bara’. This word is more powerful than the commonly used bari’ (as in إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ بَرِىٓءٌ مِّنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ - Indeed Allah is disassociated from the mushrikeen). Bara’ actually carries more weight. While bari’ can only be used for singular, bara’ encompasses singular, dual, or plural - it’s a more comprehensive, emphatic disavowal.

It’s significant that Ibrahim (AS) used this stronger term. And notice: just before this, the ayahs discussed people holding onto their ummah, their nation and ancestry. Here, uniquely, the word ummah will be used for Ibrahim (AS) alone - إِنَّ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ كَانَ أُمَّةً - Ibrahim was an ummah. Allah ﷻ is telling us between the lines: You want to commit to an ummah? Why not the ummah of Ibrahim (AS)?

مِّمَّا تَعْبُدُونَ - From what you worship. I am completely absolved. I have nothing to do with your worship.

Ayah 27

إِلَّا ٱلَّذِى فَطَرَنِى فَإِنَّهُۥ سَيَهْدِينِ

Except for the One who created me; and indeed, He will guide me.

إِلَّا ٱلَّذِى فَطَرَنِى - Except the One who fashioned me, molded me, created me in this unique, creative way. فَإِنَّهُۥ سَيَهْدِينِ - Then He, in fact, is the One who will soon guide me.

This is the declaration of pure tawheed in the face of ancestral paganism.

Ayah 28

وَجَعَلَهَا كَلِمَةًۢ بَاقِيَةً فِى عَقِبِهِۦ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ

And he made it a word remaining among his descendants that perhaps they might return [to it].

وَجَعَلَهَا كَلِمَةًۢ بَاقِيَةً - And We made it - meaning what Ibrahim (AS) said, this word of tawheed - a lasting word. فِى عَقِبِهِۦ - In his ’aqib, in his coming generations, those who came right behind him.

In other words, these words - holding onto Allah ﷻ alone and not committing shirk - Allah ﷻ instilled into the Children of Ibrahim (AS). Remember how Ibrahim (AS) told his children, just as Ya’qub (AS) did: إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ ٱصْطَفَىٰ لَكُمُ ٱلدِّينَ فَلَا تَمُوتُنَّ إِلَّا وَأَنتُم مُّسْلِمُونَ - Allah has chosen for you the religion, so do not die except as Muslims.

This commitment to the oneness of Allah ﷻ, He instilled in Ibrahim’s descendants لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ - so that they may come back. So that the rest of the people from his lineage can return to tawheed through the few who have been given prophethood.

Ayah 29

بَلْ مَتَّعْتُ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ وَءَابَآءَهُمْ حَتَّىٰ جَآءَهُمُ ٱلْحَقُّ وَرَسُولٌ مُّبِينٌ

Rather, I gave enjoyment to these [people of Makkah] and their fathers until there came to them the truth and a clear messenger.

بَلْ مَتَّعْتُ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ وَءَابَآءَهُمْ - Rather, in fact, I have provided luxury and enjoyment to these people and their ancestors. I gave them a lot. حَتَّىٰ جَآءَهُمُ ٱلْحَقُّ وَرَسُولٌ مُّبِينٌ - Until the truth came to them and a clear messenger came to them.

Ayah 30

وَلَمَّا جَآءَهُمُ ٱلْحَقُّ قَالُوا۟ هَـٰذَا سِحْرٌ وَإِنَّا بِهِۦ كَـٰفِرُونَ

But when the truth came to them, they said, “This is magic, and indeed we are, concerning it, disbelievers.”

وَلَمَّا جَآءَهُمُ ٱلْحَقُّ - And when the truth came to them, قَالُوا۟ هَـٰذَا سِحْرٌ - they said, “This is magic, sorcery.” وَإِنَّا بِهِۦ كَـٰفِرُونَ - And we are in complete denial of it. We are determined disbelievers regarding this message.

Ayah 31

وَقَالُوا۟ لَوْلَا نُزِّلَ هَـٰذَا ٱلْقُرْءَانُ عَلَىٰ رَجُلٍ مِّنَ ٱلْقَرْيَتَيْنِ عَظِيمٍ

And they said, “Why was this Quran not sent down upon a great man from [one of] the two cities?”

Their objection continues: وَقَالُوا۟ لَوْلَا نُزِّلَ هَـٰذَا ٱلْقُرْءَانُ - They said, “How come this Quran wasn’t sent down عَلَىٰ رَجُلٍ مِّنَ ٱلْقَرْيَتَيْنِ عَظِيمٍ - upon a great man from one of the two cities?”

The two cities (qaryatayn) refer to Makkah and Ta’if. These were economic capitals. Many of Makkah’s elite owned gardens and properties in Ta’if - their vacation homes, so to speak. Ta’if was naturally beautiful, situated in a valley, while Makkah held the sanctity of the Haram. These were the two wealthy centers.

Their complaint: God only had an orphan left to pick? There are so many wealthy, influential people here. When they speak, we already listen. Had they been made messengers, it would have been so much easier. Why pick him? What’s so special about Muhammad ﷺ?

Ayah 32

أَهُمْ يَقْسِمُونَ رَحْمَتَ رَبِّكَ ۚ نَحْنُ قَسَمْنَا بَيْنَهُم مَّعِيشَتَهُمْ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا ۚ وَرَفَعْنَا بَعْضَهُمْ فَوْقَ بَعْضٍ دَرَجَـٰتٍ لِّيَتَّخِذَ بَعْضُهُم بَعْضًا سُخْرِيًّا ۗ وَرَحْمَتُ رَبِّكَ خَيْرٌ مِّمَّا يَجْمَعُونَ

Is it they who would portion out the mercy of your Lord? It is We who have apportioned among them their livelihood in the life of this world and have raised some of them above others in degrees [of rank] that they may make use of one another for service. But the mercy of your Lord is better than whatever they accumulate.

Allah ﷻ responds with a powerful rhetorical question: أَهُمْ يَقْسِمُونَ رَحْمَتَ رَبِّكَ - Are they in charge of dividing the mercy of your Master?

What is “the mercy of your Master” in this context? The Quran, prophethood, revelation. They’re questioning who deserves to receive divine guidance, as if that’s their decision to make.

نَحْنُ قَسَمْنَا بَيْنَهُم مَّعِيشَتَهُمْ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا - We are the ones who divide between them their livelihood, their sustenance and luxuries in worldly life. وَرَفَعْنَا بَعْضَهُمْ فَوْقَ بَعْضٍ دَرَجَـٰتٍ - And We have elevated some over others in ranks and degrees.

The people you call “great” are not great by their own merit. I provided for them. I made them what they are. And this revelation you’re questioning? It’s not coming from a different source. The source from which this revelation comes is the same source from which the wealth of these people came. He decides. He gives some more than others. He elevates some in rank above others.

Then Allah ﷻ explains the wisdom: لِّيَتَّخِذَ بَعْضُهُم بَعْضًا سُخْرِيًّا - So that some of them can put others to work, so that some can employ others for service.

This is how industry and economies function. If everyone were a multi-billionaire, nobody would enter the lawn-mowing business or do essential services. For economies to exist, there must be those with capital - the investors, the wealth - and those who provide labor. Some people make the investments; others do the work. There are always those who hire and those who get hired. This economic diversity is by divine design.

وَرَحْمَتُ رَبِّكَ خَيْرٌ مِّمَّا يَجْمَعُونَ - And the mercy of your Lord is better than the things they gather.

The implication here is profound: what they’re gathering is wealth, material possessions. What you are receiving - the mercy of Allah ﷻ through revelation - that’s what you’re gathering. And revelation is superior to wealth. The Quran is a greater treasure than all worldly riches.

Ayah 33

وَلَوْلَآ أَن يَكُونَ ٱلنَّاسُ أُمَّةً وَٰحِدَةً لَّجَعَلْنَا لِمَن يَكْفُرُ بِٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ لِبُيُوتِهِمْ سُقُفًا مِّن فِضَّةٍ وَمَعَارِجَ عَلَيْهَا يَظْهَرُونَ

And if it were not that the people would become one community [of disbelievers], We would have made for those who disbelieve in the Most Merciful - for their houses - ceilings and stairways of silver upon which to mount.

This ayah contains a stunning statement about the worthlessness of this world in Allah’s sight.

وَلَوْلَآ أَن يَكُونَ ٱلنَّاسُ أُمَّةً وَٰحِدَةً - Had it not been the case that people would become one single nation altogether - meaning if everyone wouldn’t just follow disbelief because of worldly attractions - لَّجَعَلْنَا لِمَن يَكْفُرُ بِٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ لِبُيُوتِهِمْ سُقُفًا مِّن فِضَّةٍ - then Allah ﷻ would have made for people who disbelieve in Ar-Rahman, for their homes, He would have made roofs - ceilings - from silver.

Allah ﷻ is saying: This world and its treasures mean nothing to Me. If you were all just one nation of disbelievers, I still would have given you immense wealth. Disbelievers would have had roofs made of silver.

Consider this: roofs typically get the least expensive materials in construction - shingles, basic materials - because you’re not going to enjoy the roof. You live inside. The walls, the flooring receive the most attention and investment. The roof, the ceiling especially, gets the least attention. Yet Allah ﷻ says even the most trivial part of their construction would have been elite, made of precious metal.

وَمَعَارِجَ عَلَيْهَا يَظْهَرُونَ - And there would have been ma’arij - levels and levels and stairways upon which they would climb and rise.

They would have had multi-story mansions with elaborate staircases. In really wealthy homes, you’ll find rooms at different elevations - three steps up to another room, multiple stages rather than just straight stairs. The house unfolds in levels. This is what Allah ﷻ is describing - they would rise and descend through these elegant stages.

Ayah 34

وَلِبُيُوتِهِمْ أَبْوَٰبًا وَسُرُرًا عَلَيْهَا يَتَّكِـُٔونَ

And for their houses - doors and couches [of silver] upon which to recline.

وَلِبُيُوتِهِمْ أَبْوَٰبًا - And in their homes they would have had massive, magnificent gates and doors. وَسُرُرًا عَلَيْهَا يَتَّكِـُٔونَ - And beautiful, luxurious beds and couches upon which they would recline and relax in complete comfort.

Ayah 35

وَزُخْرُفًا ۚ وَإِن كُلُّ ذَٰلِكَ لَمَّا مَتَـٰعُ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا ۚ وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةُ عِندَ رَبِّكَ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ

And gold ornament. But all that is not but the enjoyment of worldly life. And the Hereafter with your Lord is for the righteous.

وَزُخْرُفًا - And zukhruf - all kinds of glitter, golden ornaments, shiny decorative colors, things that look like gold. Glorious, eye-catching beauty and luxury everywhere.

وَإِن كُلُّ ذَٰلِكَ لَمَّا مَتَـٰعُ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا - Notice the unique use of لَمَّا here. Typically لَمَّا means “had not yet” or “when,” but here it actually carries the meaning of إِلَّا (except/only). This is a special case in the Quran. The meaning is: And all of this is nothing but - is only - mata’ al-hayat ad-dunya, utilities and temporary enjoyment of worldly life.

The Prophet ﷺ would later describe this reality: If this world meant anything to Allah, a disbeliever wouldn’t have even the wing of a fly. Allah ﷻ is telling us in these ayahs that this world and its treasures don’t mean much to Him. They mean everything to you, but they’re insignificant to Him. He could have given unimaginable treasures to every single disbeliever if that’s what it came down to, if everyone disbelieved, because it means nothing to Him.

وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةُ عِندَ رَبِّكَ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ - And the akhirah, the Hereafter, as far as your Master is concerned, is for the people who have taqwa, who are conscious of Allah ﷻ and guard themselves against His displeasure.

This is what truly matters. This is what has value in His sight.

Ayah 36

وَمَن يَعْشُ عَن ذِكْرِ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ نُقَيِّضْ لَهُۥ شَيْطَـٰنًا فَهُوَ لَهُۥ قَرِينٌ

And whoever is blinded from remembrance of the Most Merciful - We appoint for him a devil, and he is to him a companion.

وَمَن يَعْشُ عَن ذِكْرِ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ - And whoever يَعْشُ - turns away, ignores, blinds himself from the dhikr, the remembrance and mention of Ar-Rahman.

نُقَيِّضْ لَهُۥ شَيْطَـٰنًا - We appoint for him, We assign to him a shaytan. فَهُوَ لَهُۥ قَرِينٌ - And he becomes for him a qarin - an intimate companion.

The word qarin refers to someone constantly at your side, inseparable. We’ve seen this word before in the Quran - نُقَيِّضْ لَهُۥ شَيْطَـٰنًا فَهُوَ لَهُۥ قَرِينٌ. The concept of a qarin is a shaytan assigned specifically to you, running around with you constantly.

Now we’re learning one of the primary reasons this shaytan is placed with someone: when a person deliberately turns away from and ignores the remembrance of Allah ﷻ, when they blind themselves to His guidance, then this qarin becomes their constant companion, leading them further into darkness.

This is the job, the function of this assigned shaytan - to keep that person far from the mercy of Ar-Rahman.

Ayah 36–54 · linked video: 03. Az-Zukhruf (Ayah 36-54)

Surah Az-Zukhruf: Ayahs 36-54

Ayah 36

وَمَن يَعْشُ عَن ذِكْرِ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ نُقَيِّضْ لَهُۥ شَيْطَـٰنًا فَهُوَ لَهُۥ قَرِينٌ

The word يَعْشُ means to ignore something completely, to turn away from it without paying any attention. When someone deliberately ignores the remembrance of Ar-Rahman ﷻ, Allah ﷻ responds by installing a shaytan for that person. The word نُقَيِّضْ means We put in place or establish firmly. This isn’t a random assignment—it’s a consequence. The shaytan becomes a قَرِين , an inseparable companion who is always with them.

This ayah introduces us to the concept of the qareen—the personal devil assigned to those who deliberately turn away from Allah’s remembrance. What makes this relationship particularly dangerous is revealed in the next ayah.

Ayah 37

وَإِنَّهُمْ لَيَصُدُّونَهُمْ عَنِ ٱلسَّبِيلِ وَيَحْسَبُونَ أَنَّهُم مُّهْتَدُونَ

Building on the previous ayah, we now learn what this shaytan companion actually does. These devils obstruct people from the path—they block them from guidance. But the truly insidious part is the second half of the ayah: while the shaytan takes them off the path, he convinces them that they are actually on it. They assume they are committed to guidance even as they stray further from it.

This is the great deception. The person thinks they’re living a righteous lifestyle, making good decisions, following truth—all while being led astray. The shaytan doesn’t announce himself as an enemy. He masquerades as a friend, as wisdom, as common sense, convincing the person that their deviation is actually the right path.

Ayah 38

حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا جَآءَنَا قَالَ يَـٰلَيْتَ بَيْنِى وَبَيْنَكَ بُعْدَ ٱلْمَشْرِقَيْنِ فَبِئْسَ ٱلْقَرِينُ

The word حَتَّىٰٓ means “until.” Until when? Until finally the disbeliever comes to Allah ﷻ. Throughout his life, his qareen was invisible to him, whispering, guiding, misleading. But when he comes to Allah ﷻ, the shaytan becomes visible. Now he can see his companion, and the reality of what has been done to him becomes clear.

In that moment of recognition, he cries out: يَـٰلَيْتَ بَيْنِى وَبَيْنَكَ بُعْدَ ٱلْمَشْرِقَيْنِ—“If only between you and me was the distance of the two Easts!” This is a powerful rhetorical expression. The word مَشْرِق literally means the place where the sun rises. Imagine trying to reach the place where the sun rises—it’s an impossible distance, always receding as you approach. Now imagine crossing that impossible distance, finally reaching it, and then starting all over again to cross another mashriq. Two impossible distances—that’s how far he wishes he could be from his companion.

But it’s too late. The one who was his constant companion in the world, chained to him invisibly, is now visibly bound to him. فَبِئْسَ ٱلْقَرِينُ—what a horrible companion! The word قَرِين shares the same root as قَرَن, meaning to be chained together. In Surah Al-Isra, we learn that criminals are chained together. This person spent his whole life chained to his shaytan, and now that bond is permanent.

Ayah 39

وَلَن يَنفَعَكُمُ ٱلْيَوْمَ إِذ ظَّلَمْتُمْ أَنَّكُمْ فِى ٱلْعَذَابِ مُشْتَرِكُونَ

This conversation between the person and his qareen, this wish for distance—none of it will benefit them. The ayah says: It won’t benefit you today, now that you have wronged yourselves, that you are partners in punishment. Just as you were partners in crime in the world—the shaytan whispering, you following—now you are مُشْتَرِكُونَ , partners in the punishment.

The word مُشْتَرِكُونَ emphasizes shared participation. You weren’t forced; you participated willingly. You were active partners in your own destruction, and now you will be active participants in the consequences.

Ayah 40

أَفَأَنتَ تُسْمِعُ ٱلصُّمَّ أَوْ تَهْدِى ٱلْعُمْىَ وَمَن كَانَ فِى ضَلَـٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ

Allah ﷻ now addresses the Prophet ﷺ with a rhetorical question that provides comfort in the face of rejection. Are you going to make the deaf one listen? Are you going to guide the blind one and the one who is clearly misguided?

This is directed at those whose qareen has completely taken hold of them. They’ve become spiritually deaf and blind, lost causes in their current state. The Prophet ﷺ cannot force guidance upon those who have sealed themselves off from it. This ayah provides relief from the burden of feeling responsible for those who absolutely refuse to see or hear the truth.

Ayah 41

فَإِمَّا نَذْهَبَنَّ بِكَ فَإِنَّا مِنْهُم مُّنتَقِمُونَ

Allah ﷻ now speaks directly to His Messenger ﷺ with a conditional statement. The word نَذْهَبَنَّ means to remove or take away. If We were to remove you—take you away from them, whether through death or by sending you to another town—then We will certainly take revenge from them.

This is a powerful statement. The presence of the Prophet ﷺ among the Quraysh is the only thing protecting them from immediate destruction. The moment he leaves, their protection is gone. Makkah is only surviving because the Messenger of Allah ﷺ is there. This is the pattern with prophets—when Prophet Lut (AS) left, what happened to his people? When Prophet Shu’ayb (AS) left, what happened? The Prophet’s presence is a mercy, even to those who reject him.

Ayah 42

أَوْ نُرِيَنَّكَ ٱلَّذِى وَعَدْنَـٰهُمْ فَإِنَّا عَلَيْهِم مُّقْتَدِرُونَ

Or—at the very least—We could show you what We have promised them. Allah ﷻ has the power to show the Messenger ﷺ the punishment that awaits these people, even while he’s still alive among them.

The word مُّقْتَدِرُونَ is more powerful than قَادِرُونَ. While قَادِر means having power or control, مُقْتَدِر means having absolute, complete control. No matter what they think, no matter what plans they make, no matter how secure they feel in their opposition, Allah ﷻ has complete control over them.

Ayah 43

فَٱسْتَمْسِكْ بِٱلَّذِىٓ أُوحِىَ إِلَيْكَ ۖ إِنَّكَ عَلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ

In the midst of rejection, in the face of those who cannot be guided, Allah ﷻ gives the Prophet ﷺ a direct command: فَٱسْتَمْسِكْ—hold on tightly. The word استمسك implies grasping firmly with both hands, refusing to let go. Hold on to what has been revealed to you.

When everything around you seems to be falling apart, when people mock and reject, when you feel the weight of their disbelief—don’t look at them. Don’t measure your success by their response. Hold on to the revelation. Because إِنَّكَ عَلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ—you are indeed on a straight path. They may be lost, but you are not. Your path is clear, straight, and true.

This instruction isn’t just for the Prophet ﷺ—it’s for every believer who feels surrounded by rejection of the truth.

Ayah 44

وَإِنَّهُۥ لَذِكْرٌ لَّكَ وَلِقَوْمِكَ ۖ وَسَوْفَ تُسْـَٔلُونَ

The Quran describes itself here. What is “it” in this ayah? It’s the revelation, the Quran. Allah ﷻ says: No doubt it is ذِكْر—a remembrance, a reminder, a way to remember Allah ﷻ—for you and for your nation.

This establishes something significant. Anyone who claims to be part of the nation of Muhammad ﷺ must remember Allah ﷻ through the Quran. The Quran is specifically identified as the dhikr for this Ummah.

Then comes a warning: وَسَوْفَ تُسْـَٔلُونَ—and soon all of you will be asked. Who is “all of you”? The ayah began with “you and your nation,” so the questioning includes the Prophet ﷺ and every member of his Ummah. We will all be asked. Asked about what? How much was the Quran a dhikr for you? How much did you remember Allah ﷻ through the Quran? How much did you try to memorize it, recite it, reflect on it? How much did you try to remember its teachings when you were tested?

This is our relationship with the Quran—it’s not just a book to respect and kiss. It’s meant to be our active remembrance of Allah ﷻ, and we will be questioned about that relationship.

Ayah 45

وَسْـَٔلْ مَنْ أَرْسَلْنَا مِن قَبْلِكَ مِن رُّسُلِنَآ أَجَعَلْنَا مِن دُونِ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ ءَالِهَةً يُعْبَدُونَ

Allah ﷻ now gives the Prophet ﷺ a remarkable instruction: Ask those messengers We sent before you—have We ever made gods other than Ar-Rahman that should be worshiped?

This raised an obvious question. How can the Prophet ﷺ ask messengers who came before him? He can’t meet them in this world. The answer became clear during the journey of Isra and Mi’raj, when Allah ﷻ took him to meet the previous prophets. There, he was able to ask them directly, to converse with them, and to confirm that the message has always been the same: worship of Allah ﷻ alone, with no partners.

This ayah establishes the continuity of the message. Every prophet came with the same core teaching. The denial of the Quraysh isn’t something new—it’s a continuation of the same rejection faced by messengers throughout history.

Ayah 46

وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا مُوسَىٰ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَآ إِلَىٰ فِرْعَوْنَ وَمَلَإِي۟هِۦ فَقَالَ إِنِّى رَسُولُ رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ

Having established that all messengers brought the same message, Allah ﷻ now provides a detailed example: the story of Musa (AS). We had absolutely already sent Musa with Our miracles to Fir’awn and his elite, his chiefs. The word مَلَإ refers to the leadership class, those who held power and influence around Pharaoh.

Musa (AS) came to them with a clear declaration: إِنِّى رَسُولُ رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ—“I am the messenger of the Master of all nations, the Lord of the worlds.” Notice he didn’t say “your god” or “my god”—he said رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ. This is everyone’s Master, whether they acknowledge it or not. The claim of prophethood is universal, not particular to one tribe or nation.

Ayah 47

فَلَمَّا جَآءَهُم بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَآ إِذَا هُم مِّنْهَا يَضْحَكُونَ

When he came to them with Our miracles, their immediate reaction—captured by the word إِذَا , meaning “at that very moment”—was يَضْحَكُونَ, they started laughing. This wasn’t contemplative silence or serious consideration. It was mocking, condescending laughter.

Imagine: a staff turns into a serpent, and they laugh. “That’s supposed to make me believe?” This is the response of arrogance. When people have already decided they won’t believe, even the most clear signs become objects of ridicule. They don’t engage with the evidence; they dismiss it with contempt.

Ayah 48

وَمَا نُرِيهِم مِّنْ ءَايَةٍ إِلَّا هِىَ أَكْبَرُ مِنْ أُخْتِهَا ۖ وَأَخَذْنَـٰهُم بِٱلْعَذَابِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ

We would never show them a miracle except that it would be bigger than its sister. The word أُخْت (sister) is significant. Why are miracles called sisters? When you see two sisters, you recognize they’re from the same mother because they have similarities. These miracles, though different, were clearly from the same divine source. Each new miracle was recognizably related to the previous ones but greater in impact.

Then Allah ﷻ adds: وَأَخَذْنَـٰهُم بِٱلْعَذَابِ—and We seized them with punishment. This isn’t referring to the final destruction in the sea. This refers to the nine signs that came to Fir’awn—the plagues, the locusts, the blood, the diseases. These were punishments designed لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ—so that they might return, so they might come to their senses.

Why is this story being told? Because something similar was about to happen to the Quraysh. They would face a severe drought, winds so strong they created fires, smoke enveloping the entire city of Makkah. Like the tests given to Fir’awn’s people, these would be opportunities to return to Allah ﷻ.

Ayah 49

وَقَالُوا۟ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَ ٱلسَّاحِرُ ٱدْعُ لَنَا رَبَّكَ بِمَا عَهِدَ عِندَكَ إِنَّنَا لَمُهْتَدُونَ

When the punishment became unbearable, the chiefs of Fir’awn didn’t call out to Musa (AS) by his name. They said يَـٰٓأَيُّهَ ٱلسَّاحِرُ—“O you magician!” Even in their desperation, they couldn’t bring themselves to show respect. They maintained their dismissive attitude while begging for relief.

“Call on your Lord for us,” they said, “according to what He has promised you”—meaning, You promised us relief if we believed; we’ll believe if You remove this punishment. إِنَّنَا لَمُهْتَدُونَ—“We will certainly be guided.” They promised they were ready to become Muslims if only the calamities would go away.

This is the promise of the desperate, not the commitment of the sincere.

Ayah 50

فَلَمَّا كَشَفْنَا عَنْهُمُ ٱلْعَذَابَ إِذَا هُمْ يَنكُثُونَ

The moment We removed the difficulty from them—again that word إِذَا indicating immediate action—they went back on their agreement. The word يَنكُثُونَ means to violate a promise, to break a covenant.

They had made a deal: remove these problems and we’ll become Muslims. The signs were lifted, the punishment alleviated, and immediately they returned to their disbelief. This is the nature of false promises made under duress. The second the pressure is off, the commitment disappears.

Ayah 51

وَنَادَىٰ فِرْعَوْنُ فِى قَوْمِهِۦ قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ أَلَيْسَ لِى مُلْكُ مِصْرَ وَهَـٰذِهِ ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِىٓ ۖ أَفَلَا تُبْصِرُونَ

Now Fir’awn launches his propaganda campaign. He calls out to his nation: “My people, don’t I alone own the kingdom of Egypt?” The word مُلْك indicates complete sovereignty and authority.

Then he adds: “Don’t these rivers flow beneath my feet?” This is a claim to absolute control. In an agricultural society dependent on the Nile, whoever controls the water controls everything. Fir’awn is saying: I decide which city gets water and which doesn’t. I can create drought or abundance. I have control over life and death itself.

أَفَلَا تُبْصِرُونَ—“Don’t you see?” Don’t you understand how much power I have?

This is the declaration of human sovereignty. In its pure form, sovereignty belongs only to Allah ﷻ. But here, a human being claims it for himself. This happens at the individual level with Fir’awn, but it can also happen at the societal level.

Consider the concept of democracy in its pure, ideal form—power distributed to the entire population. Instead of one Fir’awn claiming absolute authority, you have an entire population claiming it collectively. The people decide what’s right and wrong, what’s legal and illegal. In essence, you’ve taken one person who declared absolute authority and distributed that claim to everyone. The basic principle hasn’t changed: humans claiming sovereignty that belongs only to Allah ﷻ.

This doesn’t mean Islam and democratic principles cannot coexist—the truth lies somewhere in between the extremes. Islam requires, first and foremost, acknowledgment that الْحُكْمُ إِلَّا لِلَّهِ—judgment and sovereignty belong to Allah ﷻ alone. This is like a declaration of slavery to Allah ﷻ before anything else. Under that ultimate sovereignty, various forms of governance can exist.

The concept of شُورَىٰ ( consultation) in Islam shares some similarities with democratic principles, but with a critical difference. In matters where the Quran and Sunnah have established clear rulings—particularly in education and morality—these are not subject to popular vote. But in countless administrative matters—infrastructure, economic policy, urban planning—there is room for collective decision-making and consultation.

An Islamic society protects freedom of speech. We see this in the early period when Jews and Christians lived under Muslim rule. The Quran itself quotes criticisms made by People of the Book, some quite harsh. Christian ministers could come to the Prophet’s masjid and openly declare Jesus as God, and they were the Prophet’s guests. This is freedom of speech embedded in the original teachings.

The primary concerns of Islamic governance are education and morality—not to create a repressive society, but to prevent harm. For instance, pornography and alcohol may be prohibited for Muslims, but the concern extends beyond individual choice when these industries create broader social harm. The prevention of harm to society is a foundational principle of Islamic law.

The punitive laws in Islam are actually very few—crimes like murder and open zina (requiring four witnesses). Even these have flexibility; Umar (RA) put a moratorium on cutting the hand of a thief during a time of famine, recognizing that the circumstances demanded it. This shows the adaptability and mercy within Islamic governance.

But all of this begins with rejecting the claim that Fir’awn made: that ultimate authority belongs to any human being or human collective.

Ayah 52

أَمْ أَنَا۠ خَيْرٌ مِّنْ هَـٰذَا ٱلَّذِى هُوَ مَهِينٌ وَلَا يَكَادُ يُبِينُ

After asserting his own power, Fir’awn’s propaganda campaign now turns to character assassination. “Am I not better than this one”—هَـٰذَا , he doesn’t even use Musa’s name, just “this one”—“who is مَهِين , humiliated, lowly?”

He’s reminding people of Musa’s past. “Don’t you remember his track record? He ran away from this country. He killed someone. He’s a criminal.” This is a smear campaign designed to destroy credibility before addressing any arguments.

Then he adds: وَلَا يَكَادُ يُبِينُ—“and he can barely speak clearly!” Musa (AS) had a speech impediment, a slight stutter. Fir’awn mocks this. “Look, he can’t even give a proper speech. What kind of messenger is this? This is who you’re going to follow?”

This is one of the classic tactics of those in power who oppose truth: attack the messenger’s character and appearance rather than address the message. Make people laugh at the messenger so they never seriously consider what he’s saying.

Ayah 53

فَلَوْلَآ أُلْقِىَ عَلَيْهِ أَسْوِرَةٌ مِّن ذَهَبٍ أَوْ جَآءَ مَعَهُ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ مُقْتَرِنِينَ

Continuing his propaganda, Fir’awn says: “If he’s really a messenger, why haven’t bracelets of gold been thrown onto him?” In that culture, golden bracelets were a sign of royalty and authority. “Where are his symbols of power? Where’s his kingdom?”

أَوْ جَآءَ مَعَهُ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ مُقْتَرِنِينَ—“Or why don’t angels come with him, lined up in rows?” The word مُقْتَرِنِينَ suggests angels chained or bound together in formation, like a military legion. “I have armies of soldiers. Look at the gold on my hands, my bracelets, my symbols of power. What does he have?”

This argument appeals to the masses: power, wealth, and pageantry must indicate truth. A man who appears weak, who has a speech impediment, who has no army—how could he be a messenger of God?

Ayah 54

فَٱسْتَخَفَّ قَوْمَهُۥ فَأَطَاعُوهُ ۚ إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ قَوْمًا فَـٰسِقِينَ

The word اسْتَخَفَّ comes from خِفَّة , meaning lightness. But in this form, it means he made people light—he made them stupid, unable to think for themselves. He did a number on their minds.

Through his propaganda—the talk radio, the TV interviews, the negative advertisements—Fir’awn successfully made people unable to think critically. They were brainwashed. Their minds became light, easily pushed in any direction, unable to carry the weight of serious thought.

فَأَطَاعُوهُ—then they followed him. The connection between making someone’s mind light and their following is powerful. The one who can think for himself has weight, substance, gravity. He cannot be easily moved. But the one whose mind is light can be pushed this way and that, blown by every wind of rhetoric.

The ayah concludes: إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ قَوْمًا فَـٰسِقِينَ—they were a corrupt nation. Notice who is called corrupt here. Not just Fir’awn, but all those who followed him. All those who didn’t think for themselves, who accepted the propaganda without question, who allowed their minds to be made light—they are all considered corrupt by Allah ﷻ.

This is a warning about the danger of not thinking critically, of accepting narratives without examination, of allowing others to do our thinking for us. When we abdicate our responsibility to think, to question, to weigh evidence, we become complicit in our own deception. And Allah ﷻ holds us accountable for that choice.

Ayah 55–81 · linked video: 04. Az-Zukhruf (Ayah 55-81)

Surah Az-Zukhruf: Ayahs 55-81

Ayah 55

فَلَمَّآ ءَاسَفُونَا ٱنتَقَمْنَا مِنْهُمْ فَأَغْرَقْنَـٰهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ

This ayah concludes the account of Firaun and his people. Allah ﷻ states: “Then when they upset us, we avenged them and drowned all of them together.” The word آسَفُونَا carries profound meaning—when they tried to grieve Us, when they attempted to anger Allah ﷻ through their persistent rebellion and arrogance.

The response was swift and complete: فَأَغْرَقْنَـٰهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ—We drowned all of them together. This wasn’t selective punishment; it was comprehensive. When a people collectively embrace tyranny and systematically oppose the truth, their collective fate becomes sealed.

Ayah 56

فَجَعَلْنَـٰهُمْ سَلَفًا وَمَثَلًا لِّلْـَٔاخِرِينَ

The term سَلَفًا has layers of meaning. Salaf refers to something that has passed, something that is now just old history, ancient tales relegated to the past. But there’s more depth here—we made them into examples for later generations. They became a deterrent, a warning sign for those who would come after them. Their story isn’t just history; it’s a lesson preserved across time.

Ayah 57

۞ وَلَمَّا ضُرِبَ ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ مَثَلًا إِذَا قَوْمُكَ مِنْهُ يَصِدُّونَ

Allah ﷻ shifts to address a new controversy. Understanding the context is essential: the Quran had declared that everything worshiped besides Allah ﷻ would be fuel for hellfire. When the Quraysh heard this, they immediately latched onto what they perceived as a contradiction. “What about Isa (AS)?” they challenged. “People worship him, so according to you, he’ll be in hellfire too!” They began يَصِدُّونَ—raising their voices, making noise, creating commotion and distraction.

This is a critical tactic we must recognize. The Quraysh weren’t engaging in honest intellectual discourse. They were using mockery and misdirection. Some would say, “Every time the sun comes up, this guy is just preaching Christianity. You have a problem with us taking angels as daughters of God, but no problem with the son of God?” They were deliberately misquoting and misrepresenting the message, creating strawman arguments.

Notice the phrase قَوْمُكَ —“your people.” Despite their hostility, they’re still called the Prophet’s ﷺ people. He hasn’t abandoned them yet. This designation carries hope—there’s still a chance for them. Later, when the tipping point is reached, Allah ﷻ will use different language, but for now, they remain his nation.

Ayah 58

وَقَالُوٓا۟ ءَأَـٰلِهَتُنَا خَيْرٌ أَمْ هُوَ ۚ مَا ضَرَبُوهُ لَكَ إِلَّا جَدَلًۢا ۚ بَلْ هُمْ قَوْمٌ خَصِمُونَ

They said, “Are our gods better or is he?”—meaning Isa (AS). But Allah ﷻ exposes their true motivation: مَا ضَرَبُوهُ لَكَ إِلَّا جَدَلًۢا—they only brought this up for the sake of argument, for جَدَل , pointless debate without genuine inquiry.

Then comes the devastating assessment: بَلْ هُمْ قَوْمٌ خَصِمُونَ—rather, they are a people addicted to argument. The word خَصِمُونَ describes those who are perpetually contentious, who argue not to learn but to win, not to understand but to undermine. Allah ﷻ is teaching us a profound lesson: don’t engage in pointless debate with those who have no interest in truth. Recognize when someone is genuinely seeking versus when they’re simply trying to create obstacles.

Ayah 59

إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا عَبْدٌ أَنْعَمْنَا عَلَيْهِ وَجَعَلْنَـٰهُ مَثَلًا لِّبَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ

Now Allah ﷻ clarifies the truth about Isa (AS): إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا عَبْدٌ—he is nothing but a slave, a servant of Allah ﷻ. This is his honor, not his divinity. أَنْعَمْنَا عَلَيْهِ—We bestowed favor upon him. His miraculous birth, his ability to speak as an infant, his miracles—all were gifts from Allah ﷻ, not evidence of godhood.

وَجَعَلْنَـٰهُ مَثَلًا لِّبَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ—We made him an example, a sign for the Children of Israel. His very existence was meant to strengthen their faith, to demonstrate Allah’s power, yet they turned it into a source of confusion and shirk.

Ayah 60

وَلَوْ نَشَآءُ لَجَعَلْنَا مِنكُم مَّلَـٰٓئِكَةً فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ يَخْلُفُونَ

This ayah presents a powerful rhetorical point: وَلَوْ نَشَآءُ—had We willed, لَجَعَلْنَا مِنكُم مَّلَـٰٓئِكَةً—We could have made angels from among you, in the earth, يَخْلُفُونَ—succeeding one another as khalifas on earth.

The implication is striking. You human beings were given the honor of khalifa, of stewardship on earth. Had Allah ﷻ wanted, He could have given that role to angels instead. But He chose you. The miraculous birth of Isa (AS) without a father shouldn’t confuse you—Allah ﷻ creates as He wills. If He can create angels, if He can create Adam (AS) without parents, creating Isa (AS) without a father is not evidence of divinity but of divine power.

Ayah 61

وَإِنَّهُۥ لَعِلْمٌ لِّلسَّاعَةِ فَلَا تَمْتَرُنَّ بِهَا وَٱتَّبِعُونِ ۚ هَـٰذَا صِرَٰطٌ مُّسْتَقِيمٌ

The pronoun “he” in وَإِنَّهُۥ refers to Isa (AS). لَعِلْمٌ لِّلسَّاعَةِ—he is in fact a sign, real knowledge of the Hour. This has multiple meanings. First, his miraculous birth and life were signs pointing to the reality of resurrection and the Day of Judgment. But there’s also a future dimension: when Isa (AS) returns before the end of times, people will know with certainty that the Hour is approaching. His return will be convincing knowledge, undeniable proof that Judgment Day is imminent.

فَلَا تَمْتَرُنَّ بِهَا—don’t you be in any doubt about it. Don’t entertain skepticism about the Hour. وَٱتَّبِعُونِ—follow Me alone. Then comes the declaration: هَـٰذَا صِرَٰطٌ مُّسْتَقِيمٌ—this is a straight path. The path of recognizing Isa (AS) as a noble messenger but a slave of Allah ﷻ, not as divine.

Ayah 62

وَلَا يَصُدَّنَّكُمُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّ مُّبِينٌ

وَلَا يَصُدَّنَّكُمُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ—don’t let Shaytan obstruct you, don’t let him prevent you from this straight path. إِنَّهُۥ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّ مُّبِينٌ—he is certainly and absolutely a clear enemy to you. The word مُّبِينٌ emphasizes that this enmity isn’t hidden or subtle. It’s obvious, manifest, undeniable. Yet despite this clarity, Shaytan still manages to mislead through confusion about fundamental matters like the nature of prophets.

Ayah 63

وَلَمَّا جَآءَ عِيسَىٰ بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ قَالَ قَدْ جِئْتُكُم بِٱلْحِكْمَةِ وَلِأُبَيِّنَ لَكُم بَعْضَ ٱلَّذِى تَخْتَلِفُونَ فِيهِ ۖ فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُونِ

When Isa (AS) came بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ—with clear miraculous signs, he declared: قَدْ جِئْتُكُم بِٱلْحِكْمَةِ—I have come to you with wisdom. This word الْحِكْمَةِ is significant in the Quranic narrative. Musa (AS) was given the Kitab and wisdom. Dawud (AS) and Sulayman (AS) were given wisdom. The Injeel represents wisdom, just as the Tawrah represents the Book.

Notice that our Prophet Muhammad ﷺ was given both: the Quran combines both the Kitab and the Hikmah, both the Torah’s legislative clarity and the Injeel’s spiritual wisdom. This makes the Quran comprehensive in a way previous revelations were not.

Isa (AS) continues: وَلِأُبَيِّنَ لَكُم بَعْضَ ٱلَّذِى تَخْتَلِفُونَ فِيهِ—to clarify for you some of the things you’ve been disagreeing about. He came to resolve disputes, to bring clarity to confusion. Then the fundamental command: فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُونِ—be cautious of Allah’s wrath, protect yourselves from His anger, and obey me.

Ayah 64

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ رَبِّى وَرَبُّكُمْ فَٱعْبُدُوهُ ۚ هَـٰذَا صِرَٰطٌ مُّسْتَقِيمٌ

Isa (AS) declared the most fundamental truth: إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ رَبِّى وَرَبُّكُمْ—indeed Allah, He is my Master and your Master. This is the clearest possible rejection of any claim to divinity. He explicitly places himself in the same category as his followers: slaves of the same Master.

فَٱعْبُدُوهُ—so enslave yourselves to Him, worship Him alone. هَـٰذَا صِرَٰطٌ مُّسْتَقِيمٌ—this, in fact, is the straight path. The message of Isa (AS) was pure Tawheed, absolute monotheism. Any deviation from this didn’t come from him but from those who came after.

Ayah 65

فَٱخْتَلَفَ ٱلْأَحْزَابُ مِنۢ بَيْنِهِمْ ۖ فَوَيْلٌ لِّلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ مِنْ عَذَابِ يَوْمٍ أَلِيمٍ

Despite this clarity, فَٱخْتَلَفَ ٱلْأَحْزَابُ مِنۢ بَيْنِهِمْ—groups among them fell into disagreement. They splintered into factions, each claiming to follow Isa (AS) while contradicting his core message.

فَوَيْلٌ لِّلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟—then woe, terrible destruction for those who did wrong. What was their wrong? Distorting the message, attributing divinity to a prophet who explicitly proclaimed his servitude. مِنْ عَذَابِ يَوْمٍ أَلِيمٍ—from the punishment of a painful day. The word أَلِيمٍ emphasizes that this isn’t just punishment; it’s pain that penetrates, that cannot be escaped or numbed.

Ayah 66

هَلْ يَنظُرُونَ إِلَّا ٱلسَّاعَةَ أَن تَأْتِيَهُم بَغْتَةً وَهُمْ لَا يَشْعُرُونَ

هَلْ يَنظُرُونَ إِلَّا ٱلسَّاعَةَ—are they waiting around until the Hour itself shows up? The question is rhetorical, highlighting their heedlessness. أَن تَأْتِيَهُم بَغْتَةً—that it arrives at them suddenly, attacks them out of nowhere. وَهُمْ لَا يَشْعُرُونَ—while they won’t have any realization whatsoever. They’ll be completely unprepared, caught off guard by what they spent their lives denying.

Ayah 67

ٱلْأَخِلَّآءُ يَوْمَئِذٍۭ بَعْضُهُمْ لِبَعْضٍ عَدُوٌّ إِلَّا ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ

ٱلْأَخِلَّآءُ—close friends, best friends. The word khalil represents one of the closest forms of friendship in Arabic. Ibrahim (AS) is called Khalilullah, the intimate friend of Allah ﷻ. This term describes the deepest bonds of companionship.

يَوْمَئِذٍۭ بَعْضُهُمْ لِبَعْضٍ عَدُوٌّ—on that day, they will be enemies to one another. The closest friends in this world, if their friendship was based on anything other than righteousness, will turn on each other. They’ll blame each other, curse each other, wish punishment upon each other.

إِلَّا ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ—except those who had taqwa. The people of taqwa, those whose friendship was built on consciousness of Allah ﷻ, will remain friends. Their bond transcends this world because it was rooted in something eternal.

Ayah 68

يَـٰعِبَادِ لَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْكُمُ ٱلْيَوْمَ وَلَآ أَنتُمْ تَحْزَنُونَ

يَـٰعِبَادِ—O My slaves! This direct address from Allah ﷻ is extraordinary. Usually, we learn that angels will comfort believers, saying “don’t fear, don’t grieve.” But here, Allah ﷻ Himself speaks directly to them.

لَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْكُمُ ٱلْيَوْمَ—there is no fear upon you today. On the day when everyone else is terrified, when the earth is shaking and hearts are trembling, believers hear these words of comfort directly from their Master.

وَلَآ أَنتُمْ تَحْزَنُونَ—nor are you the ones who will grieve. These words are in the second person, a direct conversation. No sound will be more comforting on that day than Allah ﷻ saying: you have nothing to fear. This is an additional honor beyond all others, a privilege reserved for those who maintained their relationship with Him through all trials.

Ayah 69

ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا وَكَانُوا۟ مُسْلِمِينَ

Who are these blessed slaves? ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا—those who believed in Our revelations, Our signs, Our miracles. They saw the evidence and accepted it with open hearts.

وَكَانُوا۟ مُسْلِمِينَ—and they had been Muslims. The verb كَانُوا indicates continuity—they were Muslims consistently, not just in moments of ease but throughout their lives. Islam wasn’t a temporary state for them; it was their permanent identity.

Ayah 70

ٱدْخُلُوا۟ ٱلْجَنَّةَ أَنتُمْ وَأَزْوَٰجُكُمْ تُحْبَرُونَ

The conversation that began with “O My slaves” continues. ٱدْخُلُوا۟ ٱلْجَنَّةَ—enter Jannah. This is Allah ﷻ Himself saying “enter.” Not an angel announcing it, but Allah ﷻ personally welcoming His slaves into eternal bliss.

أَنتُمْ وَأَزْوَٰجُكُمْ—you and your spouses. The righteous enter together with their righteous companions. تُحْبَرُونَ—you have been made happy beyond happiness. The word حبر describes extreme happiness, joy so complete that nothing can disturb it, satisfaction beyond all dissatisfaction.

This is the passive form تُحْبَرُونَ, which indicates that this happiness wasn’t even possible for you before. It’s a form of joy that has been specially instilled, a divine gift of contentment that transcends anything experienced in this world. You’ve been made happy beyond the possibility of sadness.

Ayah 71

يُطَافُ عَلَيْهِم بِصِحَافٍ مِّن ذَهَبٍ وَأَكْوَابٍ ۖ وَفِيهَا مَا تَشْتَهِيهِ ٱلْأَنفُسُ وَتَلَذُّ ٱلْأَعْيُنُ ۖ وَأَنتُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ

يُطَافُ عَلَيْهِم بِصِحَافٍ—servants go around them with صحاف , gigantic trays. A صحفة is a massive tray or plate that holds multiple dishes, the kind that traditionally an entire family would gather around to eat from together. These aren’t individual servings; they’re lavish presentations of abundance.

مِّن ذَهَبٍ—made of gold. The containers themselves are treasures. وَأَكْوَابٍ—and large cups, goblets of precious materials.

وَفِيهَا مَا تَشْتَهِيهِ ٱلْأَنفُسُ—and in it is whatever the souls desire. You’ll have cravings, and whatever you genuinely want to drink, to taste, to experience will be present in these vessels.

وَتَلَذُّ ٱلْأَعْيُنُ—and what delights the eyes. The word لذ typically refers to delicious taste for the tongue, but here it’s applied to sight. Allah ﷻ acknowledges that visual beauty matters, that our eyes crave aesthetic pleasure. In this world, people design their homes, choose colors carefully, select cars based on appearance. We spend entire industries on what’s pleasing to the eye—interior decoration, fashion, design. In Jannah, our eyes will “taste” beauty. We’ll look at things and the visual pleasure will be so intense it’s like our eyes are experiencing flavor.

وَأَنتُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ—and in that you will permanently remain. This joy isn’t temporary, isn’t followed by loss or decline. It’s eternal.

Ayah 72

وَتِلْكَ ٱلْجَنَّةُ ٱلَّتِىٓ أُورِثْتُمُوهَا بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ

وَتِلْكَ ٱلْجَنَّةُ—and that, in fact, is the ultimate paradise. ٱلَّتِىٓ أُورِثْتُمُوهَا—which you have inherited. The concept of inheritance is beautiful here. You didn’t purchase it; you inherited it. It was prepared for you, designated for you, waiting for you.

بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ—because of what you used to do. Your actions in the temporary world secured your inheritance in the eternal one. The deeds weren’t payment—Jannah can never be earned—but they were the means by which Allah ﷻ chose to grant you this inheritance.

Ayah 73

لَكُمْ فِيهَا فَـٰكِهَةٌ كَثِيرَةٌ مِّنْهَا تَأْكُلُونَ

لَكُمْ فِيهَا فَـٰكِهَةٌ كَثِيرَةٌ—for you in it are abundant fruits, fruits in vast variety and quantity. مِّنْهَا تَأْكُلُونَ—from which you will eat. The mention of fruits specifically, after all the golden plates and goblets, emphasizes natural pleasure, wholesome satisfaction. It’s not just luxury; it’s genuine nourishment and delight.

Ayah 74

إِنَّ ٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ فِى عَذَابِ جَهَنَّمَ خَـٰلِدُونَ

Now the scene shifts dramatically. إِنَّ ٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ—indeed the criminals, the guilty ones. فِى عَذَابِ جَهَنَّمَ خَـٰلِدُونَ—in the punishment of Jahannam they will permanently remain. Just as the believers are خَـٰلِدُونَ in Jannah, the criminals are خَـٰلِدُونَ in hellfire. Permanence works both ways.

Ayah 75

لَا يُفَتَّرُ عَنْهُمْ وَهُمْ فِيهِ مُبْلِسُونَ

لَا يُفَتَّرُ عَنْهُمْ—it will not be lessened for them. The word فترة means a span of time, a break, a pause. There will be no recess, no intermission, no “we’ll be right back after this commercial break.” The punishment continues without pause, without relief.

وَهُمْ فِيهِ مُبْلِسُونَ—and in it they are completely hopeless. The word مُبْلِسُونَ comes from إبليس , and it describes someone who has lost all hope, who sees no possibility of escape or improvement. Complete despair has set in.

Ayah 76

وَمَا ظَلَمْنَـٰهُمْ وَلَـٰكِن كَانُوا۟ هُمُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ

وَمَا ظَلَمْنَـٰهُمْ—We did not wrong them. This declaration is significant. Allah ﷻ asserts that their punishment isn’t injustice; it’s justice. They weren’t victims of divine tyranny.

وَلَـٰكِن كَانُوا۟ هُمُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ—but they, they themselves were the wrongdoers. The emphasis هُمُ isolates them as the sole source of their misery. They wronged themselves through their choices, their rejection of truth, their persistence in evil.

Ayah 77

وَنَادَوْا۟ يَـٰمَـٰلِكُ لِيَقْضِ عَلَيْنَا رَبُّكَ ۖ قَالَ إِنَّكُم مَّـٰكِثُونَ

وَنَادَوْا۟ يَـٰمَـٰلِكُ—and they will call out: “O Malik!” Malik is the angel in charge of hellfire, the head guardian, the chief warden. In their desperation, they appeal to him.

لِيَقْضِ عَلَيْنَا رَبُّكَ—tell your Master to just kill us already, to finish us off, to end this existence. They’re begging for annihilation, for non-existence, anything to escape the pain.

قَالَ إِنَّكُم مَّـٰكِثُونَ—he says: you will remain waiting. The word مَّـٰكِثُونَ is devastating. It means to stay somewhere waiting, like when Musa (AS) told his family to stay and wait for his return from the mountain. They will wait for relief that never comes, for an end that never arrives. Even this prayer for death will go unanswered.

Ayah 78

لَقَدْ جِئْنَـٰكُم بِٱلْحَقِّ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَكُمْ لِلْحَقِّ كَـٰرِهُونَ

After depicting this scene of hellfire, Allah ﷻ explains the reason. لَقَدْ جِئْنَـٰكُم بِٱلْحَقِّ—We had certainly brought you the truth. The message came clearly, undeniably.

وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَكُمْ لِلْحَقِّ كَـٰرِهُونَ—but most of you were disgusted by the truth, repelled by it. The word كَـٰرِهُونَ indicates active hatred, not passive disinterest. When truth came, you didn’t just ignore it; you were revolted by it. You turned away in disgust.

Ayah 79

أَمْ أَبْرَمُوٓا۟ أَمْرًا فَإِنَّا مُبْرِمُونَ

Now comes a powerful rhetorical device. أَمْ أَبْرَمُوٓا۟ أَمْرًا—have they tied up the rope? Have they secured their decision?

The word إبرام refers to the strongest kind of rope used in construction. In traditional building, planks of wood weren’t nailed together; they were tied with thick, coiled rope doubled for strength. When you tied such a rope, you made the most secure knot possible because lives depended on it. You tied it with the intention of never undoing it.

This is a metaphor for their decision to remain in disbelief. Have they tied up their decision with such a strong knot that they’re never going back on it? Are they that sure they want to remain disbelievers?

فَإِنَّا مُبْرِمُونَ—then We too have tied up Our knot. We’ve made Our decision firm as well. If you’re certain about rejection, then I’m certain about sending you to what was just described.

Notice the sequence: أَبْرَمُوٓا۟ is in the past tense, but مُبْرِمُونَ is ongoing and permanent. Even though they’re sure now, on Judgment Day they’ll wish they had been Muslims. But Allah’s decision will be final, irreversible. There will be no going back.

This ayah teaches us that Allah ﷻ doesn’t want to send people to hellfire. His decision is conditional—it’s a consequence of their decision. The far (then) indicates cause and effect: they made their choice first, and only then did Allah ﷻ make His. But once He makes it, it’s permanent.

Ayah 80

أَمْ يَحْسَبُونَ أَنَّا لَا نَسْمَعُ سِرَّهُمْ وَنَجْوَىٰهُم ۚ بَلَىٰ وَرُسُلُنَا لَدَيْهِمْ يَكْتُبُونَ

أَمْ يَحْسَبُونَ أَنَّا لَا نَسْمَعُ—do they assume that We don’t hear? سِرَّهُمْ—their secrets, their personal, private thoughts and plans. وَنَجْوَىٰهُم—and their secret councils, their collective scheming. Najwa refers to when multiple people hold a secret together, when they’re plotting in groups.

The disbelievers would hold secret meetings about how to attack Islam, how to undermine the message, what strategy to employ next. They thought these councils were hidden from divine knowledge.

بَلَىٰ—oh no, not at all! We hear everything. وَرُسُلُنَا لَدَيْهِمْ يَكْتُبُونَ—and Our messengers—meaning the angels—are in their company writing everything down. We get full intelligence briefings on all their secret meetings. Nothing is hidden, nothing is missed. Every whisper is documented, every plot is recorded.

Ayah 81

قُلْ إِن كَانَ لِلرَّحْمَـٰنِ وَلَدٌ فَأَنَا۠ أَوَّلُ ٱلْعَـٰبِدِينَ

This final ayah requires careful understanding because it uses a rhetorical form unique to Arabic that can be misunderstood in translation.

قُلْ—say, O Muhammad ﷺ. إِن كَانَ لِلرَّحْمَـٰنِ وَلَدٌ—if the Most Merciful had a child. فَأَنَا۠ أَوَّلُ ٱلْعَـٰبِدِينَ—then I would be the first of the worshipers.

This statement has been misused by opponents of Islam. Christians have said, “See? He’s saying if God had a son, he would worship him. Well, He does!” Even some heretical groups within Islamic history, like the Qadianis, have tried to use this linguistic principle to argue that prophethood is still open because there’s a hadith where the Prophet ﷺ said, “If Ibrahim [his son] had lived, he would have been a prophet.”

But this completely misunderstands the rhetorical principle. In Arabic, when you want to emphasize that something is absolutely impossible, you put yourself on the line. You make the most extreme statement about yourself to prove your conviction. It’s as if the Prophet ﷺ is saying: “My life and my conviction are worth staking on this. If this utterly impossible thing were true, I would be the first to act on it—but it’s not true, and that’s exactly my point.”

This is why the ayah says فَأَنَا۠ أَوَّلُ—I would be the first. The Prophet ﷺ is demonstrating that he’s more committed to truth than anyone. If there were any truth to this claim about Allah ﷻ having a child, he would be the first to recognize it. But there isn’t, and his categorical rejection proves his absolute certainty.
