This rhetorical device appears throughout Arabic literature. It doesn’t open a door to the possibility; it slams it shut with the weight of personal conviction. The Prophet ﷺ is saying: I stake everything on the impossibility of this claim. If it were true—which it absolutely is not—I would be the first to acknowledge it. But since I’m not acknowledging it, you know with certainty that it’s false.

May Allah ﷻ grant us the wisdom to understand His words correctly and the strength to follow the straight path demonstrated by all His messengers, from Ibrahim (AS) to Isa (AS) to Muhammad ﷺ.

Ayah 81–89 · linked video: 05. Az-Zukhruf (Ayah 81-89)

Surah Az-Zukhruf: Ayahs 81-89

Ayah 81

قُلْ إِن كَانَ لِلرَّحْمَـٰنِ وَلَدٌ فَأَنَا۠ أَوَّلُ ٱلْعَـٰبِدِينَ

This ayah presents a powerful rhetorical argument against those who attribute a son to Allah ﷻ. The Prophet ﷺ is commanded to say: if Ar-Rahman truly had a child, then I would be the first to worship that child. But the reality is clear—this is an absolute impossibility. The structure of the statement itself reveals its absurdity. It’s not even a real conditional scenario because such a thing can never exist.

The beauty of this response lies in its certainty. The Prophet ﷺ is declaring that he would never worship a supposed child of Allah ﷻ because there is no such thing. The phrase “I would be the first” emphasizes complete rejection through irony—he’s saying this will never happen, so the question of being first doesn’t even arise.

Ayah 82

سُبْحَـٰنَ رَبِّ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ رَبِّ ٱلْعَرْشِ عَمَّا يَصِفُونَ

After dismantling their false claim, Allah ﷻ immediately declares His perfection: He is far above and beyond any such attribution. SubhanAllah—perfect is the Master of the heavens and the earth, the Master of the Throne, far above what they describe.

Notice the progression here. Allah ﷻ is not just described as the Master of creation, but specifically as رَبِّ ٱلْعَرْشِ —the Master of the Throne itself. This is the highest level of authority and dominion. The word عَمَّا means “above” or “beyond,” emphasizing how far removed Allah’s reality is from their false descriptions. Whatever attributions they make, whatever limitations they try to place on Allah ﷻ, He is infinitely above and beyond all of it.

Ayah 83

فَذَرْهُمْ يَخُوضُوا۟ وَيَلْعَبُوا۟ حَتَّىٰ يُلَـٰقُوا۟ يَوْمَهُمُ ٱلَّذِى يُوعَدُونَ

Now comes a command to the Prophet ﷺ that requires tremendous patience and restraint: leave them alone. Let them engage in their خَوْض —their idle talk, their gossip, their useless debates. The word khaud carries the meaning of wading through shallow water aimlessly, talking just to talk, discussing matters without seriousness or depth. It’s conversation meant to waste time, to turn everything into a joke.

These people aren’t genuinely seeking guidance or engaging in sincere religious inquiry. They’re trying to get under your skin, to provoke you, to tangle you up in endless debates where they can watch you get worked up. The command is clear: don’t engage with that. يَلْعَبُوا۟ —let them play around, treat serious matters lightly, make mockery of what deserves respect.

But this isn’t permission without consequence. حَتَّىٰ يُلَـٰقُوا۟ يَوْمَهُمُ —until they come into contact with their Day, the Day that has been promised to them. That meeting is inevitable. Right now they’re playing games, but the Day of Judgment is coming, and on that day, the reality will become undeniable.

Ayah 84

وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى فِى ٱلسَّمَآءِ إِلَـٰهٌ وَفِى ٱلْأَرْضِ إِلَـٰهٌ ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلْحَكِيمُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ

This ayah addresses a concept familiar to the Christian community that was part of the audience for this message. It echoes the language of the Lord’s Prayer—“Thy kingdom come, in heaven as it is on Earth.” Allah ﷻ declares: He is the One worthy of worship in the sky and the One worthy of worship on the earth. His authority, His divinity, His dominion extends everywhere.

It’s important to understand that السَّمَآءِ in Arabic doesn’t just mean “sky” in a limited sense. The word sama without the plural form (samawat) carries the meaning of “above” in an unlimited sense. When we say samawat (the seven heavens), we’re placing a limit—seven layers of what is above. But when Allah ﷻ uses the singular sama here, He’s not limiting Himself. He is the One above, without restriction or boundary, and the One in the earth. His authority encompasses everything, everywhere.

The ayah concludes with two of His beautiful names: Al-Hakeem (the Ultimately Wise) and Al-’Aleem (the All-Knowing). Every decision He makes, every command He gives, every aspect of His dominion is based on perfect wisdom and complete knowledge.

Ayah 85

وَتَبَارَكَ ٱلَّذِى لَهُۥ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا وَعِندَهُۥ عِلْمُ ٱلسَّاعَةِ وَإِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ

The word تَبَارَكَ is profound and deserves our attention. We’ve discussed the concept of barakah in detail before, but two essential meanings should always remain clear in our minds. First, barakah means an increase of good beyond what is expected—more benefit, more goodness, more blessing than anticipated. Second, it means extracting potential goodness from something that contains it. Just as a seed cannot release its goodness until water touches it, making that water mubarak (blessed), Allah’s barakah brings out the hidden potential in all of creation.

How full of barakah is the One who owns the dominion of the heavens and the earth! His kingdom encompasses السَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا (the heavens, the earth, and everything between them). Nothing exists outside His ownership and control.

Then comes a critical declaration: وَعِندَهُۥ عِلْمُ ٱلسَّاعَةِ —and He alone possesses the knowledge of the Hour. No one else knows when Judgment Day will come. This knowledge belongs exclusively to Him. وَإِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ —and to Him alone will all of you be returned. Every soul, every creation, will return to Him for accounting.

Ayah 86

وَلَا يَمْلِكُ ٱلَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِهِ ٱلشَّفَـٰعَةَ إِلَّا مَن شَهِدَ بِٱلْحَقِّ وَهُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ

Those beings they call upon besides Allah ﷻ—whether idols, angels, prophets, or any created thing—do not possess the power of الشَّفَـٰعَةَ , intercession. They cannot make a case before Allah ﷻ on anyone’s behalf.

But notice the exception: إِلَّا مَن شَهِدَ بِٱلْحَقِّ —except for the one who testifies with truth. And here’s the striking reality: the one who testifies with truth will not be making a case in their favor, but against them. We’ve already encountered this concept in the Quran—your own skin will testify against you, your hands will testify against you. The only testimony they’ll find on that Day is true testimony, and it will not work in their favor. وَهُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ —and they know this very well.

Ayah 87

وَلَئِن سَأَلْتَهُم مَّنْ خَلَقَهُمْ لَيَقُولُنَّ ٱللَّهُ ۖ فَأَنَّىٰ يُؤْفَكُونَ

This ayah reveals a stunning contradiction in their belief system. If you were to ask them, “Who created you?”—they would absolutely, without hesitation, say “Allah ﷻ.” They acknowledge His creative power. They recognize that He brought them into existence.

So the question becomes: فَأَنَّىٰ يُؤْفَكُونَ —then how are they turned away? How are they deluded? How can they acknowledge Allah ﷻ as their Creator yet worship others besides Him? The word يُؤْفَكُونَ carries the meaning of being turned away from the truth, being deluded, being made to deviate. If they know Allah ﷻ is their Creator, how can they be so thoroughly confused about who deserves their worship?

Ayah 88

وَقِيلِهِۦ يَـٰرَبِّ إِنَّ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ قَوْمٌ لَّا يُؤْمِنُونَ

This ayah requires careful attention because of its weight and significance. Allah ﷻ says: وَقِيلِهِۦ —and by his statement. The pronoun here refers to the Prophet ﷺ. Allah ﷻ is taking an oath by the words of His Messenger ﷺ.

The word قِيل is related to قَوْل , which means statement or speech. It’s similar to the structure we see elsewhere in the Quran when Allah ﷻ swears by something. Here, Allah ﷻ swears by the testimony of His Prophet ﷺ when he says: يَـٰرَبِّ إِنَّ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ قَوْمٌ لَّا يُؤْمِنُونَ —“My Master, indeed these are a people who do not believe.”

Now consider the depth of what’s happening here. In the previous ayah, we learned that the only one who can testify with truth is the one whose testimony will be accepted. And here, the Prophet ﷺ will be testifying—and Allah ﷻ swears by that testimony—that these people refused to believe.

Remember what we learned earlier in this surah: one of the main reasons the Quraysh haven’t been destroyed is because the Prophet ﷺ is still among them. His presence is their protection. If he were removed, they would face immediate annihilation. The very person who is the reason for their survival in this world is the very person who will testify against them on the Day of Judgment. If Allah ﷻ would have destroyed them in the dunya simply for their disbelief, what will He do when His own Messenger ﷺ testifies against them on the Day of Judgment? This is tremendously heavy.

Ayah 89

فَٱصْفَحْ عَنْهُمْ وَقُلْ سَلَـٰمٌ ۚ فَسَوْفَ يَعْلَمُونَ

Despite the gravity of what was just revealed—despite the fact that the Prophet ﷺ will testify against these people—look at the beautiful transformation at the end of the surah. Allah ﷻ commands: فَٱصْفَحْ عَنْهُمْ —so turn the page on them. Ignore it. Leave them alone.

The command اصْفَحْ means to turn away, to overlook, to not let their behavior affect you. Even though their rejection is so severe that the Messenger of Allah ﷺ will swear an oath testifying against them, right now is not the time for that reckoning. Right now, you have a different job: deliver the message and be patient with them.

وَقُلْ سَلَـٰمٌ —and just say “peace” to them. Maintain peaceful relations. Don’t let their insults, their mockery, their blasphemy get to you. This is an incredibly high expectation. These people are insulting the Prophet ﷺ, insulting the Quran, attributing daughters to Allah ﷻ—blasphemy that would boil any believer’s blood. Can you imagine what the Prophet ﷺ felt? His belief in these matters was infinitely stronger than ours, yet he’s commanded to let it go, to not let it affect him, to develop a thick skin and simply maintain peace with them.

Notice the contrast: at the beginning of this surah, Allah ﷻ asked, أَفَنَضْرِبُ عَنكُمُ ٱلذِّكْرَ صَفْحًا —“Should We then turn away the Reminder from you as an excuse?” Should We give up on you because you’re so lost in transgression? The answer was no—Allah ﷻ will not turn the message away. But when people become extremely arrogant and obnoxious, what should the Messenger do? Turn the page. Don’t engage with their provocations.

فَسَوْفَ يَعْلَمُونَ —soon they will know. Right now, the Prophet ﷺ places words of peace on his tongue. But on the Day of Judgment, the words won’t be words of peace. They’ll be words of testimony, words that confirm their rejection and seal their fate. But for now: peace. Soon they’ll discover what it means to insult the messengers of Allah ﷻ.

This warning held true for the Prophet ﷺ in dealing with the disbelievers of Makkah, and it remains true for us when we encounter people who hate Islam, who insult our faith, who attack our Prophet ﷺ and our Book. We’re dealing with the same dynamic—people who despise the message, who mock what we hold sacred, who say things that make our blood boil. But the command is the same: let it go. Don’t let it consume you. Grow a thick skin. Maintain peaceful relations with them.

These expectations began in Surah Fussilat, continued through Surah Ash-Shura, and now reach their culmination here. But this surah adds something additional: a reminder that while they may be enjoying themselves now, the Day of Judgment is coming. If they’ve made up their minds and refuse to believe, they will face the worst punishment. And what is that worst punishment? That the Prophet ﷺ himself testifies against them. But for now, don’t worry about it. Allah ﷻ says: soon I’ll deal with them. Soon enough, they’ll know.
